title: Ovarian Cancer
oneLiner: Insidious postmenopausal cancer presenting with non-specific abdominal symptoms; high mortality due to late diagnosis; treatable but often recurrent.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** (reproductive age) – positive urine β-hCG + haemodynamic instability ± peritonism → **surgical emergency** (not cancer workup)
- **Ovarian torsion** (any age) – acute severe unilateral pelvic pain + vomiting + absent Doppler flow on TVUS → **urgent gynaecology referral** (salvage ovary)
- **Massive pulmonary embolism** (due to thromboembolism) – sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, raised JVP, hypotension → **immediate CT pulmonary angiogram** if high clinical suspicion
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms** (often vague, >3 months):
  - Abdominal bloating/distension (most common)
  - Early satiety / loss of appetite
  - Pelvic or lower abdominal pain/pressure
  - Urinary urgency/frequency
  - Change in bowel habit (constipation > diarrhoea)
  - Unexplained weight loss, fatigue
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable pelvic/adnexal mass (bimanual exam)
  - Ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
  - Omental cake (firm nodular mass in upper abdomen)
  - Pleural effusion (reduced breath sounds, stony dullness)
  - Signs of cachexia / ECOG performance status decline
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Benign ovarian cyst** – anechoic simple cyst on TVUS, thin wall, no septations; premenopausal > postmenopausal
- **Endometrioma** – ground-glass echogenicity, chronic cyclical pain; CA-125 may be mildly raised
- **Fibroids** – uterine irregularity, heavy menstrual bleeding; TVUS shows uterine origin
- **Colorectal cancer** – rectal bleeding, change in bowel habit, positive FOB; CA-19-9 / CEA elevated
- **Primary peritoneal cancer** – identical presentation to ovarian but no discrete ovarian mass; requires surgical confirmation
- **Tubo-ovarian abscess** – fever, severe pelvic pain, elevated CRP/WCC; requires antibiotics/ drainage
- **Splitter:** Transvaginal ultrasound morphology – simple cyst+lacks solid components → benign; complex cyst with septations/papillary projections/solid areas → malignancy until proven otherwise
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine or serum β-hCG (STAT) – rule out ectopic in reproductive age
  - Abdominal ultrasound (if ascites, assess for free fluid)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia of chronic disease, infection), U&E (renal), LFTs (hepatic metastases), CRP, CA-125 (not diagnostic – use in RMI)
  - If GI primary suspected: CEA, CA-19-9
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)** – morphology index
  - Second-line: **CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast** – staging (chest, liver, peritoneum, lymph nodes)
  - MRI pelvis (if TVUS equivocal or to assess resectability)
- **Special tests:**
  - Ascitic tap (if present) – send for cytology, biochemistry, culture
  - If CT suspicious: ultrasound-guided biopsy of omental cake or mass (if not surgical candidate)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **RMI (Risk of Malignancy Index):** U (ultrasound score 0-3) × M (menopausal status 1 or 3) × CA-125 (U/L)
  - RMI >200 → high risk of malignancy → urgent gynaecology oncology referral (NICE)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine β-hCG** – omission in reproductive-age female with adnexal mass can miss life-threatening ectopic pregnancy
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute pain + shock + haemoperitoneum → crash call, IV access, crossmatch, urgent laparoscopy/laparotomy
  - If stable: analgesia, NBM pending imaging, refer to gynaecology
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Complete staging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, tumour markers (CA-125, CEA, CA-19-9), MDT discussion
  - Manage ascites: therapeutic paracentesis if tense; consider diuretics only after ruling out malignant aetiology (no strong evidence)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) – malignancy + pelvic surgery → high risk
  - Nutritional assessment ± dietitian involvement (ascites, early satiety)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Surgery: **total abdominal hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy + omentectomy + lymphadenectomy** (if fit, stages I–III)
  - Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (advanced stage III–IV): 3–4 cycles of carboplatin+paclitaxel, then interval debulking
  - Adjuvant chemotherapy after surgery (if high risk)
  - Maintenance: PARP inhibitors (olaparib) if BRCA1/2 mutation present
- **Default Orders (if admitted for surgery):**
  - **Fluids:** IV Hartmann’s 1L/8h (1.5L D5W/0.45% NaCl can be used if Na+ high) – [check local protocol]
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (or tinzaparin 4500U SC OD) starting pre‑op
  - **Diet:** NBM from midnight; clear fluids until 2h pre‑op
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4‑hourly; if ascites/pleural effusion → consider O2 saturations ± ABG
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line chemotherapy (adjuvant/neoadjuvant):**
  - Carboplatin AUC 5–6 IV (dose by Calvert formula using CrCl) + Paclitaxel 175 mg/m² IV over 3 hours – every 21 days × 6 cycles [follow local chemotherapy protocol]
  - Premedication for paclitaxel: dexamethasone 20 mg IV (or oral 12h and 6h prior), chlorphenamine 10 mg IV, ranitidine 50 mg IV (or equivalent H2 antagonist) – [BNF dosing available]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Carboplatin alone (mono) in elderly/frail – [check local policy]
  - If hypersensitivity to paclitaxel: docetaxel 75 mg/m² IV every 21 days (premed similar)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Carboplatin: avoid if CrCl <20 mL/min (consider alternate or reduced dose)
  - Paclitaxel: metabolism via CYP2C8/CYP3A4 – caution with strong inhibitors (e.g. ketoconazole, grapefruit)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin: dose reduction for CrCl 20–50 mL/min (Calvert formula mandatory)
  - Paclitaxel: no fixed dose change for mild hepatic impairment; avoid if bilirubin >5× ULN or transaminases >10× ULN
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Carboplatin overdose** due to incorrect CrCl calculation (e.g. using actual body weight instead of adjusted) → severe myelosuppression, bleeding, sepsis. Always double‑check Calvert formula with senior or pharmacy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Bowel obstruction (tumour deposits on bowel serosa) – NBM, NG tube, surgical review if closed loop
  - Ascites → respiratory compromise – therapeutic paracentesis
  - Pleural effusion (malignant) – ultrasound-guided drainage
  - Chemotherapy: neutropenic sepsis (fever ≥38°C, centre, urgent septic screen + IV antibiotics per local policy), hypersensitivity reaction (during paclitaxel: bronchospasm, hypotension → stop infusion, give adrenaline)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence within 2–3 years (high risk)
  - Peripheral neuropathy (taxanes) – dose reduction or switch
  - Bowel dysfunction after surgery (ileostomy, obstruction)
  - Lymphedema (legs)
  - Psychological distress / depression
sections.Prognosis:
- 5‑year overall survival ~45% (all stages); Stage I >90%, Stage IV <20%
- Majority present at advanced stage (III/IV) due to symptom insensitivity
- Initial response to chemotherapy high (>80%) but recurrence is common; prognosis depends on surgical cytoreduction and platinum sensitivity
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do urine β-hCG** in any woman 15–50 with an adnexal mass – missing ectopic is a serious error
- **Calculate RMI** for all postmenopausal women with a pelvic mass – guides urgent referral to gynaecology oncology
- **Send ascitic fluid for cytology** before empirically treating with diuretics – a positive result secures the diagnosis
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not ignore** persistent bloating, early satiety, or urinary frequency in a postmenopausal woman as “IBS” – this is the classic diagnostic delay
- **Do not use CA-125 alone** to rule in/out ovarian cancer – it is non‑specific (raised in endometriosis, PID, peritonitis, benign cysts); always combine with imaging and menopausal status
- **Do not start diuretics** for ascites without investigating the cause – malignant ascites requires paracentesis/chemotherapy, not diuretic therapy
- **Do not give chemotherapy** without verifying renal function for carboplatin dosing – the Calvert formula is life-saving; check CrCl properly
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Severe new abdominal/pelvic pain (suspicion of torsion, rupture, obstruction)
  - Vomiting or inability to tolerate oral intake (obstruction)
  - Dyspnoea or pleuritic chest pain (PE/pleural effusion)
  - Fever ≥38°C or rigors (especially if on chemo – possible neutropenic sepsis)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Gynaecology oncology clinic review within 2 weeks of referral
  - Genetic counselling referral if <50 years or family history of breast/ovarian cancer (BRCA testing)
  - If on chemotherapy: ensure day 21 cycle schedule, contact chemotherapy helpline for any adverse effects
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspected ovarian torsion or rupture (acute severe pain, peritonism, haemodynamic instability)
  - Bowel obstruction not resolving after NG tube and conservative measures
  - Septic shock – fever, hypotension, tachycardia (especially in chemo patient)
- **Crash call (2222) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <8
  - Hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) despite high-flow O2
  - Anaphylaxis during paclitaxel infusion
- **ICU referral:** persistent organ failure (renal, respiratory, circulatory) or need for vasopressors

rawMarkdown:
## Ovarian Cancer
**One-liner:** Insidious postmenopausal cancer presenting with non-specific abdominal symptoms; high mortality due to late diagnosis; treatable but often recurrent.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** (reproductive age) – positive urine β-hCG + haemodynamic instability ± peritonism → **surgical emergency** (not cancer workup)
- **Ovarian torsion** (any age) – acute severe unilateral pelvic pain + vomiting + absent Doppler flow on TVUS → **urgent gynaecology referral** (salvage ovary)
- **Massive pulmonary embolism** (due to thromboembolism) – sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, raised JVP, hypotension → **immediate CT pulmonary angiogram** if high clinical suspicion

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms** (often vague, >3 months):
  - Abdominal bloating/distension (most common)
  - Early satiety / loss of appetite
  - Pelvic or lower abdominal pain/pressure
  - Urinary urgency/frequency
  - Change in bowel habit (constipation > diarrhoea)
  - Unexplained weight loss, fatigue
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Palpable pelvic/adnexal mass (bimanual exam)
  - Ascites (shifting dullness, fluid thrill)
  - Omental cake (firm nodular mass in upper abdomen)
  - Pleural effusion (reduced breath sounds, stony dullness)
  - Signs of cachexia / ECOG performance status decline

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Benign ovarian cyst** – anechoic simple cyst on TVUS, thin wall, no septations; premenopausal > postmenopausal
- **Endometrioma** – ground-glass echogenicity, chronic cyclical pain; CA-125 may be mildly raised
- **Fibroids** – uterine irregularity, heavy menstrual bleeding; TVUS shows uterine origin
- **Colorectal cancer** – rectal bleeding, change in bowel habit, positive FOB; CA-19-9 / CEA elevated
- **Primary peritoneal cancer** – identical presentation to ovarian but no discrete ovarian mass; requires surgical confirmation
- **Tubo-ovarian abscess** – fever, severe pelvic pain, elevated CRP/WCC; requires antibiotics/ drainage
- **Splitter:** Transvaginal ultrasound morphology – simple cyst+lacks solid components → benign; complex cyst with septations/papillary projections/solid areas → malignancy until proven otherwise

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine or serum β-hCG (STAT) – rule out ectopic in reproductive age
  - Abdominal ultrasound (if ascites, assess for free fluid)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia of chronic disease, infection), U&E (renal), LFTs (hepatic metastases), CRP, CA-125 (not diagnostic – use in RMI)
  - If GI primary suspected: CEA, CA-19-9
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)** – morphology index
  - Second-line: **CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast** – staging (chest, liver, peritoneum, lymph nodes)
  - MRI pelvis (if TVUS equivocal or to assess resectability)
- **Special tests:**
  - Ascitic tap (if present) – send for cytology, biochemistry, culture
  - If CT suspicious: ultrasound-guided biopsy of omental cake or mass (if not surgical candidate)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **RMI (Risk of Malignancy Index):** U (ultrasound score 0-3) × M (menopausal status 1 or 3) × CA-125 (U/L)
  - RMI >200 → high risk of malignancy → urgent gynaecology oncology referral (NICE)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine β-hCG** – omission in reproductive-age female with adnexal mass can miss life-threatening ectopic pregnancy

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute pain + shock + haemoperitoneum → crash call, IV access, crossmatch, urgent laparoscopy/laparotomy
  - If stable: analgesia, NBM pending imaging, refer to gynaecology
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Complete staging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, tumour markers (CA-125, CEA, CA-19-9), MDT discussion
  - Manage ascites: therapeutic paracentesis if tense; consider diuretics only after ruling out malignant aetiology (no strong evidence)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) – malignancy + pelvic surgery → high risk
  - Nutritional assessment ± dietitian involvement (ascites, early satiety)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Surgery: **total abdominal hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy + omentectomy + lymphadenectomy** (if fit, stages I–III)
  - Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (advanced stage III–IV): 3–4 cycles of carboplatin+paclitaxel, then interval debulking
  - Adjuvant chemotherapy after surgery (if high risk)
  - Maintenance: PARP inhibitors (olaparib) if BRCA1/2 mutation present
- **Default Orders (if admitted for surgery):**
  - **Fluids:** IV Hartmann’s 1L/8h (1.5L D5W/0.45% NaCl can be used if Na+ high) – [check local protocol]
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (or tinzaparin 4500U SC OD) starting pre‑op
  - **Diet:** NBM from midnight; clear fluids until 2h pre‑op
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4‑hourly; if ascites/pleural effusion → consider O2 saturations ± ABG

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line chemotherapy (adjuvant/neoadjuvant):**
  - Carboplatin AUC 5–6 IV (dose by Calvert formula using CrCl) + Paclitaxel 175 mg/m² IV over 3 hours – every 21 days × 6 cycles [follow local chemotherapy protocol]
  - Premedication for paclitaxel: dexamethasone 20 mg IV (or oral 12h and 6h prior), chlorphenamine 10 mg IV, ranitidine 50 mg IV (or equivalent H2 antagonist) – [BNF dosing available]
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Carboplatin alone (mono) in elderly/frail – [check local policy]
  - If hypersensitivity to paclitaxel: docetaxel 75 mg/m² IV every 21 days (premed similar)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Carboplatin: avoid if CrCl <20 mL/min (consider alternate or reduced dose)
  - Paclitaxel: metabolism via CYP2C8/CYP3A4 – caution with strong inhibitors (e.g. ketoconazole, grapefruit)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin: dose reduction for CrCl 20–50 mL/min (Calvert formula mandatory)
  - Paclitaxel: no fixed dose change for mild hepatic impairment; avoid if bilirubin >5× ULN or transaminases >10× ULN
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Carboplatin overdose** due to incorrect CrCl calculation (e.g. using actual body weight instead of adjusted) → severe myelosuppression, bleeding, sepsis. Always double‑check Calvert formula with senior or pharmacy.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Bowel obstruction (tumour deposits on bowel serosa) – NBM, NG tube, surgical review if closed loop
  - Ascites → respiratory compromise – therapeutic paracentesis
  - Pleural effusion (malignant) – ultrasound-guided drainage
  - Chemotherapy: neutropenic sepsis (fever ≥38°C, centre, urgent septic screen + IV antibiotics per local policy), hypersensitivity reaction (during paclitaxel: bronchospasm, hypotension → stop infusion, give adrenaline)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence within 2–3 years (high risk)
  - Peripheral neuropathy (taxanes) – dose reduction or switch
  - Bowel dysfunction after surgery (ileostomy, obstruction)
  - Lymphedema (legs)
  - Psychological distress / depression

### Prognosis
- 5‑year overall survival ~45% (all stages); Stage I >90%, Stage IV <20%
- Majority present at advanced stage (III/IV) due to symptom insensitivity
- Initial response to chemotherapy high (>80%) but recurrence is common; prognosis depends on surgical cytoreduction and platinum sensitivity

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do urine β-hCG** in any woman 15–50 with an adnexal mass – missing ectopic is a serious error
- **Calculate RMI** for all postmenopausal women with a pelvic mass – guides urgent referral to gynaecology oncology
- **Send ascitic fluid for cytology** before empirically treating with diuretics – a positive result secures the diagnosis

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not ignore** persistent bloating, early satiety, or urinary frequency in a postmenopausal woman as “IBS” – this is the classic diagnostic delay
- **Do not use CA-125 alone** to rule in/out ovarian cancer – it is non‑specific (raised in endometriosis, PID, peritonitis, benign cysts); always combine with imaging and menopausal status
- **Do not start diuretics** for ascites without investigating the cause – malignant ascites requires paracentesis/chemotherapy, not diuretic therapy
- **Do not give chemotherapy** without verifying renal function for carboplatin dosing – the Calvert formula is life-saving; check CrCl properly

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Severe new abdominal/pelvic pain (suspicion of torsion, rupture, obstruction)
  - Vomiting or inability to tolerate oral intake (obstruction)
  - Dyspnoea or pleuritic chest pain (PE/pleural effusion)
  - Fever ≥38°C or rigors (especially if on chemo – possible neutropenic sepsis)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Gynaecology oncology clinic review within 2 weeks of referral
  - Genetic counselling referral if <50 years or family history of breast/ovarian cancer (BRCA testing)
  - If on chemotherapy: ensure day 21 cycle schedule, contact chemotherapy helpline for any adverse effects

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**
  - Suspected ovarian torsion or rupture (acute severe pain, peritonism, haemodynamic instability)
  - Bowel obstruction not resolving after NG tube and conservative measures
  - Septic shock – fever, hypotension, tachycardia (especially in chemo patient)
- **Crash call (2222) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <8
  - Hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) despite high-flow O2
  - Anaphylaxis during paclitaxel infusion
- **ICU referral:** persistent organ failure (renal, respiratory, circulatory) or need for vasopressors
